title: Picture this! Central bank visuals across five continents
summary: This ECB Blog article surveys how central banks worldwide use visual communications—infographics, illustrations, videos, and plain language—to explain monetary policy to the general public. It highlights examples from the Bank of Uganda, Bank of Namibia, Bank of Japan, Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas, Reserve Bank of New Zealand, Banco Central de Chile, Banco Central de Paraguay, Bank of Jamaica, Bank of England, and the ECB itself. The article notes that consumer inflation expectations matter as much as financial market expectations for monetary policy transmission.
key_points: Central banks are increasingly using visual communications to explain current monetary policy decisions to the general public, not just financial professionals
Research cited indicates consumer inflation expectations matter 'just as much, if not more, than those of financial markets for the transmission of monetary policy'
The ECB explicitly committed in its 2021 strategy review to offering 'layered and visualised versions of monetary policy communication'
Bank of Uganda publishes its Monetary Policy Statements in both English and Luganda, the country's most widely spoken local language
Bank of Jamaica produces 'Inflation Watch' videos televised by the national broadcaster to explain monthly price movements
The article is a descriptive survey of communication practices; it contains no monetary policy decisions, rate guidance, or economic data
why_it_matters: This article describes central bank communication strategies rather than providing market-relevant macro information. It offers no data on policy rates, balance sheets, inflation prints, GDP figures, or any quantitative inputs for market analysis. It is not relevant for trading, risk management, or investment research.
